solutions.title: Tutorial
solutions.content:
1431I - Cyclic Shifts
There are several solutions to the problem, even unexpected ones, like trie merging. But let's describe the author's solution.
Let's suppose to make the string query $$$t$$$ appear we made $$$k$$$ cyclic shifts of suffixes $$$1  \lt  i_1  \lt  i_2  \lt  \dots  \lt  i_k \le m$$$. It equivalent to building $$$t$$$ as concatenation of substrings $$$s_{r_1}[1 \dots i_1 - 1]$$$ from some row $$$r_1$$$, $$$s_{r_2}[i_1 \dots i_2 - 1]$$$ from other row $$$r_2$$$ and so on.
Let's define a string on row $$$r$$$ starting from column $$$c$$$ as $$$s_{r,c}$$$ and character on row $$$r$$$ and column $$$c$$$ as $$$ch_{r,c}$$$.
It's not hard to prove that there is a greedy strategy: at first, you should find such row $$$r_1$$$ that $$$s_{r_1, 1}$$$ and $$$t$$$ has the maximum common prefix (let's name its length as $$$l_1$$$). Then we "cut" this prefix from $$$t$$$ and find $$$r_2$$$ that $$$s_{r_2, 1 + l_1}$$$ and $$$t$$$ has the maximum common prefix and so on. If we found the whole string $$$t$$$ then the answer is the number of segments minus one, otherwise, it's impossible to build $$$t$$$.
The question is how to find these common prefixes. To do it, we can sort all $$$s_{r, c}$$$ in each column $$$c$$$ lexicographically in the following way:
if $$$c = m$$$, then $$$s_{r,c} = ch_{r, c}$$$, and we can sort strings manually;
otherwise, we know that $$$s_{r,c} = ch_{r,c} + s_{r, c + 1}$$$, so we assign to $$$s_{r,c}$$$ a pair $$$(ch_{r,c}, \text{index of $$$s_{r, c + 1}$$$ in lexicographical order})$$$ and we can sort these pairs instead.
In total, complexity of sorting is $$$O(nm \log(n))$$$.
Now, for starting column $$$c$$$ we need to find the longest common prefix of $$$t$$$ and any $$$s_{i, c}$$$. Since we already sorted $$$s_{i, c}$$$, we know that all strings starting from character $$$t_1$$$ form a segment in it and we can binary search its left and right border $$$[l_1, r_1]$$$. Then all strings starting from two characters $$$t_1 t_2$$$ form a subsegment $$$[l_2, r_2] \subseteq [l_1, r_1]$$$, so we can binary search $$$l_2$$$ and $$$r_2$$$ inside segment $$$[l_1, r_1]$$$.
In other words, we can binary search the first character $$$t_1$$$ in all rows, then binary search the second character $$$t_2$$$ inside the segment $$$[l_1, r_1]$$$ and so on while the segment is not empty. In total, we make $$$O(m)$$$ binary searches per query $$$t$$$ with total complexity $$$O(m \log(n))$$$.
The total complexity is $$$O((n + q)m \log(n))$$$.

solutions.title: Solution (Ne0n25)

import

.
io
.
PrintWriter
fun main
()
{
val
(
n
,
m
,
q
)
=
readLine
()!!.
split
(
' '

map
{
it
.
toInt
()
}
val s
=
Array
(
n
)
{
readLine
()!!
}
val c
=
Array
(
m
+
1
)
{
IntArray
(
n
)
{
0
}

=
Array
(
m
+
1

{
IntArray
(
n
)
{
0
}

for
(

in
m
-
1
downTo
0
)
{
val cur
=
Array
(
n
)
{
intArrayOf
(

[
it
][
j

toInt
(),
c
[
j
+
1
][
it
],
it
)

cur
.

(

({
it
[
0
]},
{
it
[
1
]}))

(
i
in
0
until
n
)
rc
[

][
i
]
=
cur
[
i
][
2
]
c
[
j
][
cur
[
0
][
2
]]
=
0
for
(
i
in
1
until
n
)
{
val
add
=
if
(
cur
[
i
][
0
]
==
cur
[
i
-
1
][
0
]
&&
cur
[
i
][
1
]
==
cur
[
i
-
1
][
1
])
0
else
1
c
[
j
][
cur
[
i
][
2
]]
=
c
[
j
][
cur
[
i
-
1
][
2
]]
+
add
}
}
val writer
=
PrintWriter
(
System
.
out
)
repeat
(
q
)
{
val t
=
readLine
()!!
var
ans
=
0
var
j
=
0
while
(
j
<
m
)
{
var
(
nj
,
L
,
R
)
=
intArrayOf
(
j
,
0
,
n
-
1
)
while
(
nj
<
m
)
{
var
(
l1
,
r1
,
nL
)
=
intArrayOf
(
L
,
R
,
R
+
1
)
while
(
l1
<=
r1
)
{
val mid
=
(
l1
+
r1
)
/
2
if
(
s
[
rc
[
j
][
mid
]][
nj
]
>=
t
[
nj
])
{
nL
=
mid
						r1
=
mid
-
1

else
{
l1
=
mid
+
1
}
}
var
(
l2
,
r2
,
nR
)
=

(
nL
,
R
,

-
1

(

<=

)

(
l2
+

/
2
if
(

[

[

][

]][

]

[

])

nR
=
mid
						l2
=

+
1

{
r2
=
mid
-
1
}

if
(
nL
>

)

L
=
nL
				R
=
nR
++
nj
}
if
(
j
==
nj
)
{

=
0

ans
+=
1
j
=

.

(
